plants.nativeContext: Native to the rainforest understorey of Peru and neighbouring South America, carpeting damp, shaded forest floors.
plants.notes: Classic beginner closed-terrarium plant. Wilts dramatically when dry but recovers quickly with water. Avoid direct light.

plants.nativeContext: A spikemoss from the humid subtropical forests of southern China.
plants.notes: Iridescent blue-green foliage. Needs consistently high humidity; thrives in sealed containers. Spreads as a low ground cover.
Extremely forgiving. Attach to wood or rocks or use as ground cover. Tolerates a wide temperature range.

plants.nativeContext: Native to shaded, damp rocks on islands of the western Mediterranean.
plants.notes: Tiny-leaved creeping ground cover. Can spread aggressively; trim regularly. Prefers cooler temps than most tropical species.
Attractive silvery-green patterned leaves. Trails nicely over substrate or rocks. Not a true begonia despite the common name.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid tropical rainforests of Brazil, on the shaded forest floor.
plants.notes: Leaves fold upward at night. Sensitive to fluoride in tap water; use rainwater or distilled. Watch for spider mites.

plants.nativeContext: From the warm, humid rainforests of Colombia and Ecuador, in deep shade beneath the canopy.
plants.notes: Striking pink-striped foliage. Highly sensitive to drafts, cold, and mineral-heavy water. Rewarding in a stable sealed environment.
Fast grower — pinch tips regularly to stay compact. Leggy growth in low light. Loses colour intensity without adequate indirect light.

plants.nativeContext: From humid, shaded ravines and waterfall splash zones in tropical South America.
plants.notes: Delicate fan-shaped leaflets. Drops fronds immediately if humidity drops. One of the most demanding ferns; excels in sealed vivariums.

plants.nativeContext: An epiphytic fern of tropical Asian and Pacific rainforests, perched in the forks of canopy trees.
plants.notes: Bold, glossy strap-shaped fronds. More tolerant than delicate ferns. Avoid water sitting in the crown. Gets large — best in bigger builds.
Deeply textured, bronze-green leaves. Easy to propagate from cuttings. Good mid-layer plant for closed builds.

plants.nativeContext: An aquatic fern from the streams and riverbanks of Southeast Asia, attached to rocks and wood.
plants.notes: Attach rhizome to wood or rock — do not bury it in substrate. Propagates via plantlets that form on older fronds.
Metallic copper-green foliage with orange-red flowers. Needs warmth; cold drafts cause leaf drop. Spreads via stolons.

plants.toxicity: Contains insoluble calcium oxalate crystals — chewing or ingesting causes intense mouth and throat irritation, drooling, and vomiting in pets and people. Keep away from curious pets and children.
plants.notes: Trailing or climbing habit. Trim regularly to keep scale manageable in enclosed builds. Very adaptable and forgiving.
Miniature-growing pleurothallid orchid. Prefers good airflow even in high humidity. Mount on cork bark or fine sphagnum for best results.
Textured rippled leaves. Semi-succulent stems store some water; allow top of substrate to dry slightly between watering.
Delicate trailing stems with small round leaves. Excellent for cascading over edges of backgrounds or wood.

plants.nativeContext: A terrestrial bromeliad from the shaded forest floors of eastern Brazil.
plants.notes: Star-shaped rosette bromeliad. Holds water in its central cup; do not let cup go dry. Pups freely after flowering.
Grass-like tufts; stays very compact. Excellent as a ground-cover accent. Very cold-tolerant — one of the few options for cool builds.

plants.toxicity: Foliage and sap carry calcium oxalate crystals; ingestion irritates the mouth and digestive tract and the sap can irritate skin. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.notes: Arrow-shaped leaves on compact juvenile growth. Trim frequently; mature climbing growth is not suitable for enclosed builds.
Fast-spreading carpet plant with tiny round leaves. Easy to control with trimming. Good filler for open midground areas.

plants.toxicity: Begonias contain soluble calcium oxalates, most concentrated in the tubers; ingestion can cause mouth irritation, drooling, and vomiting in cats and dogs.
plants.notes: Spectacular metallic, patterned foliage. High humidity is essential but avoid water on leaves — promotes botrytis. Needs bright indirect light.

plants.nativeContext: From shaded, rocky ground in the Eastern Cape of South Africa, often sheltered under shrubs.
plants.notes: Tolerates low light better than most succulents. White warty stripes on leaves. Prefers bright indirect rather than direct sun — windows without afternoon sun are ideal.

plants.nativeContext: From semi-arid rocky outcrops in the highlands of Mexico, in bright sun and fast-draining ground.
plants.notes: Classic rosette succulent. Needs bright direct sun to maintain compact form; etiolates quickly in shade. Pups prolifically.
Bean-shaped leaves turn red at tips with sun stress. Stems are brittle — handle carefully. Leaves propagate easily.

plants.toxicity: Jade is toxic to cats and dogs; ingestion can cause vomiting, lethargy, and incoordination. Keep away from pets.
plants.notes: Dwarf jade stays compact for years. Water only when completely dry. Good for rock-garden style open terrariums.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the forested foothills of the Himalayas in Yunnan, China.
plants.notes: Round pancake-shaped leaves. Rotate regularly for even growth. Pups freely at the base. Prefers cooler temperatures than tropical species.

plants.toxicity: The sap causes contact dermatitis in pets and can irritate sensitive skin in people; ingestion may cause mild digestive upset. Listed toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.notes: Very fast-growing trailing plant. Can overwhelm smaller companions; trim frequently. Purple-leaf varieties need brighter light for colour retention.
Forms dense, velvety light-green clumps. Perfect for micro containers and jars as a primary ground cover.
Feathery aquatic moss that grows beautifully on damp wood or rocks. Excellent choice for miniature sealed setups.
Features furry surface rhizomes that resemble animal feet. Thrives in both closed or well-ventilated open terrariums.
Graceful fern that grows miniature plantlets directly on its fronds. Prefers stable, highly humid closed systems.
Stunning leaves patterned exactly like watermelon rinds. Semi-succulent stems make it sensitive to overwatering in closed glass.
Thick, glossy leaves that store moisture. Highly durable choice for larger open terrariums or monitored closed ones.
An elegant slow grower with dark metallic hues. Must be attached to rock or wood rather than buried; ideal for micro sealed jars.
Extremely robust miniature aquatic plant. Thrives perfectly in waterlogged, hyper-humid conditions on wood or hardscape inside tiny jars.
Produces small clover-like leaves on fast-growing creeping runners. Forms a dense green carpet if trimmed regularly.
A beautiful trailing plant with red stems and tiny metallic blue-silver leaves. Spreads quickly and looks magnificent cascading down.

plants.toxicity: Pothos contains insoluble calcium oxalates; ingestion causes oral irritation, drooling, and vomiting. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.notes: A scaled-down cultivar of the incredibly tough pothos vine. Highly adaptable, handles trailing or climbing with ease.
An amphibious species whose leaves flush attractive reddish colors under bright light. Excels in swampy closed layouts.
Grows in triangular, fern-like branch structures resembling a pine tree. Highly forgiving and spectacular for miniature setups.
Even smaller and more compact than standard Baby Tears. Forms an ultra-dense microscopic bright green carpet.

plants.nativeContext: An epiphytic jungle cactus of tropical American rainforests, dangling from tree branches.
plants.notes: An epiphytic jungle cactus that prefers indirect light. Tolerates well-monitored closed terrariums surprisingly well.

plants.nativeContext: From the winter-rainfall fynbos of South Africa's Western Cape, in permanently damp, nutrient-poor seeps.
plants.notes: Forgiving beginner carnivore. Use only rain/distilled water — minerals are lethal. Wants bright light and airflow; dews drop in stale air.

plants.nativeContext: A highland pitcher plant endemic to the Philippines, on mossy mountain slopes in cool, humid cloud forest.
plants.notes: A tolerant highland Nepenthes that pitchers well in humid enclosures. Never let the airy, chunky mix stay soggy. Distilled water only.

plants.nativeContext: Native to rocky, seasonally dry slopes in the mountains of Mexico and Central America.
plants.notes: Sticky-leaved gnat catcher with a mineral, near-neutral mix (unusual for a carnivore). Has a drier winter succulent phase — ease off water then.
Compact, lemon-scented button fronds. One of the easiest ferns for both open and closed setups; keep evenly moist.
Variegated cretan brake fern. Tolerates a touch of lime, so a neutral mix is fine. Airy fronds suit medium-sized planted terraria.
Puckered, crocodile-skin fronds. An epiphyte at heart — loves humidity and a chunky, never-waterlogged root run.
Chartreuse carpeting spikemoss. Needs constant humidity — crisps fast in open air. Excellent foreground groundcover in sealed jars.
Flat carpeting moss that knits into a lawn over substrate and hardscape. Mist to establish; thrives on the high humidity of a closed lid.
Forms plush cushions ("mood" = it flexes with humidity). Wants shade and a sealed, humid microclimate; dislikes being buried or sat in water.

plants.toxicity: An aroid containing calcium oxalate crystals; ingestion irritates the mouth and digestive tract. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: From the rainforests of southern Thailand and Malaysia, climbing tree trunks toward the light.
plants.notes: Fast fenestrated climber for larger open terraria — give it a moss pole or it sprawls. Too vigorous for small sealed jars.

plants.toxicity: An aroid containing calcium oxalate crystals; sap can irritate skin and ingestion irritates the mouth and gut. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: A small epiphytic aroid of Central and South American rainforests, perched on branches and trunks.
plants.notes: A small epiphytic anthurium with pearly berries. Wants an airy, bark-rich mix and high humidity — ideal climbing a background in a closed tank.

plants.toxicity: Peace lily contains insoluble calcium oxalate crystals; ingestion irritates the mouth and throat and causes drooling and vomiting. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid rainforest floor of Central and South America.
plants.notes: Low-light tolerant and a useful humidity gauge (it droops when dry). A compact specimen suits medium terraria; remove once it outgrows.

plants.toxicity: Begonias contain soluble calcium oxalates, most concentrated in the tubers; ingestion can cause mouth irritation, drooling, and vomiting in cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: From the dim understorey of Malaysian montane rainforest, where its leaves shimmer blue in low light.
plants.notes: Iridescent blue leaves (structural colour in low light). A true terrarium begonia — needs sealed humidity and shade to keep the shimmer.

plants.toxicity: Begonias contain soluble calcium oxalates, most concentrated in the tubers; ingestion can cause mouth irritation, drooling, and vomiting in cats and dogs.
plants.notes: Silver-spotted angel-wing begonia. Prefers airflow over a sealed lid to avoid mildew; best as a centrepiece in an open terrarium.
Epiphytic trailer with tubular red blooms. Let it cascade from the rim of an open container; an airy epiphyte mix keeps the roots happy.
Long-blooming gesneriad for open, airy terraria. Water the substrate, not the crown, and let the top dry slightly between waterings.

plants.toxicity: String of pearls is toxic if ingested — causing drooling, vomiting, and lethargy in pets — and the sap can irritate skin. Mildly toxic to people.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the dry scrublands of south-western Africa, trailing over rock in the shade of larger plants.
plants.notes: Bead-like trailing succulent for the edge of an open dish. Hates humidity and wet feet — strictly open, gritty, and dry between soaks.

plants.nativeContext: From the arid gravel plains of southern Africa, mimicking surrounding stones to escape grazing.
plants.notes: Mimicry succulents that look like pebbles. Need direct sun, a mineral alkaline grit, and near-zero water in winter. Open containers only.

plants.nativeContext: Native to arid limestone hills in central Mexico.
plants.notes: Clustering thimble cactus whose offsets drop and root easily. Bright sun and a sharp, lime-tolerant mix; never enclose — rot is certain.

plants.nativeContext: From shaded cliffs in southern Mexico, where its stems trail down rock faces.
plants.notes: Trailing rope of plump blue-green leaves that shed at a touch — site it where it won't be brushed. Open, dry, and bright.

plants.toxicity: Ficus sap (a milky latex) can irritate skin and the mouth; ingestion may cause mild digestive upset. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: From the forests and rocky slopes of East Asia, climbing tree trunks and walls.
plants.notes: Tiny-leaved self-clinging vine that carpets backgrounds and hardscape. Vigorous — pinch often or it blankets everything in a closed tank.
Neither strawberry nor begonia — a hardy rosette that throws plantlets on runners. Cooler-growing; happy in open or lidded setups.
Silver-splashed quilted leaves on an easy, bushy grower. Pinch tips to keep it dense; prefers airflow to a fully sealed lid.

plants.toxicity: The sap causes contact dermatitis in pets and can irritate sensitive skin in people; ingestion may cause mild digestive upset. Listed toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.notes: Fast purple-striped trailer — nearly unkillable and great for quick coverage in open terraria. Cut back hard whenever it gets leggy.
